citation.title: Adapting Human Leadership Approaches for Role Allocation in Human-Robot Navigation Scenarios

extraction_metadata.notes: Single-human/single-robot HRI (not a swarm system): the HumAnS-3D testbed controls one virtual robot. Included per the batch request; flagged here for the user's awareness given this project's swarm focus.

study_type.justification: Human participants performed a real navigation task under four conditions with measured execution time and NASA-TLX workload, making User Study Primary. The article's other major, comparably-weighted contribution is a new conceptual mapping of human-organizational leadership styles (directive/transactional/transformational/empowering) onto human-robot task/goal attributes (Tables II-IV), a genuinely new classification scheme, warranting Framework/Taxonomy Paper as Secondary.

summary.task: Participants operated a single virtual robot (HumAnS-3D 3-D virtual test environment, also linked to a physical robot platform) through a 10x10 grid containing 3 target blocks to visit and 5 obstacle blocks to avoid, under four conditions crossing leadership style (directive vs. transactional) with time constraint (time-critical, 4-minute limit, vs. time-available, unlimited).
summary.control: In the directive condition, participants directly teleoperated the robot's movement using Forward/Back/Turn buttons. In the transactional condition, participants instead periodically selected 2 of the 5 top-ranked candidate paths generated by a real-time genetic algorithm (whose fitness-function weighting adapted based on the operator's prior selections), rather than directly steering the robot.
summary.participants: Number of participants is not explicitly stated in the article — referred to only generically as the 'human participation set,' with execution time and NASA-TLX workload normalized per participant across an unspecified total number of participants (k); no demographic detail is given.
summary.results: The directive leadership style produced significantly faster task-execution times than the transactional style but at the cost of higher operator workload (NASA-TLX). Adding a time-critical constraint increased performance by 12.5% on average but also increased workload by 18.5%. The transactional style, by contrast, reduced operator workload relative to directive control.
summary.issues: The paper flags an increased human-error risk associated with the higher workload produced by directive/time-critical control, citing workload as 'perhaps the greatest contributor to human error in many systems.' No sample size, statistical tests, or significance values are reported for the four-scenario performance/workload comparisons, which are presented only as normalized descriptive bar-chart values.
summary.recommendations: Match leadership/interaction style to task context: use directive control when execution speed is paramount and higher operator workload is tolerable (e.g., crisis/time-critical, well-defined tasks), and use transactional (algorithm-assisted, delegation-style) control when minimizing operator workload matters more than raw speed. Future work should validate and compare the two remaining leadership styles (transformational and empowering) not tested in this paper.
summary.autonomous_capabilities: In the transactional condition, the robot's candidate paths are computed by a real-time genetic algorithm (10 chromosomes/candidate paths of 20 grid-point genes each) whose fitness function combines target-reaching, path-distance-minimization, and area-coverage objectives, autonomously generating and ranking candidate paths each generation; the operator's path selections dynamically re-weight the fitness function for the next generation, but the algorithm otherwise computes and proposes full-path solutions without the operator micromanaging individual robot movements.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: The 3 target blocks and 5 obstacle blocks are placed once at task initialization and appear visible on the display from the start (Figure 2a shows the full environment at initialization), so they are multiple, static, known-location instances rather than either scattered-and-intermittently-arising (Distributed) or initially-unknown-and-discovered (Static/Fixed) — the same known-location gap flagged in bashyal_2008_radiation_source_hsi and cummings_2008_mauve_controller_capacity, here extended to a multi-instance case.

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: Visiting the 3 known-location target blocks is a navigate-to-a-known-point goal, not a Search (location isn't unknown) or Engagement (no decisive/neutralizing action beyond arrival) task — the same gap flagged in bashyal_2008_radiation_source_hsi.
Participants must avoid the 5 obstacle blocks while navigating to targets, the canonical avoidance pattern of steering around a negative-consequence object while still accomplishing the primary task.

team_size.justification: Each participant operates exactly one virtual/linked robot; this is not a multi-robot or swarm system.

team_composition.justification: With only one human and one robot involved, homogeneity/heterogeneity does not apply on either side.

interaction_model.justification: A single operator controls or directs exactly one robot.

interaction_directness.justification: The operator's teleoperation commands and path selections are explicitly addressed to the single robot.

communication_proximity.justification: The operator interacts through the HumAnS-3D screen-based virtual environment interface, not physically co-located with the robot.

coordination_architecture.justification: Only a single robot is present, so multi-robot coordination does not conceptually apply.

computational_characteristics.justification: In the directive condition the robot's motion is produced entirely by the operator's direct teleoperation commands (Forward/Back/Turn), with no robot-side computation of its own path or behavior.

A genetic algorithm (population of candidate paths, fitness function, iterative generational update) computes and ranks candidate navigation paths in the transactional condition, the codebook's canonical example of this code's mechanism.

human_involvement_level.justification: Direct teleoperation requires continuous human control input; robot motion stops without it.
The genetic algorithm's fitness-function weighting and path selection require the operator's periodic top-2 path choice each generation to proceed and converge as intended.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: The operator continuously sets the robot's exact movement via Forward/Back/Turn button presses.
The operator gives a discrete input (selecting 2 of 5 candidate paths) as a route parameter; the robot's own navigation then executes the chosen path.

operational_domain.justification: The robot navigates a ground-based grid environment.

flags.note: Multiple target/obstacle blocks are placed once and visible from task start, not discovered via search and not arising intermittently — extends the 'known fixed target' gap to a multi-instance case.
flags.proposed_code: See bashyal_2008_radiation_source_hsi flag for 'Known Fixed Task Object' — this article suggests the proposed code should also explicitly accommodate multiple simultaneous known-location instances, not just a single one.

flags.note: Visiting known-location target blocks is a navigate-to-known-target behavior, the same gap flagged in bashyal_2008_radiation_source_hsi.
Direct teleoperation in the directive condition involves no robot-side computation of motion at all.
